statement.markdown: Given two strings A and B, print the longest common subsequence (LCS) of A and B. If there are multiple longest common subsequences, print any one of them.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers representing the lengths of A and B respectively.
The second line contains string A.
The third line contains string B.
statement.outputFormat: A single line containing the longest common subsequence.

examples.explanation: The longest common subsequence is BD.
The longest common subsequence is GTAB.

hints.text: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming. Think about how you can use a 2D array to store the lengths of common subsequences.
The 2D array can be filled in a bottom-up manner. For each cell, consider whether the current characters in sequences A and B match, and update the cell accordingly.
To print the LCS itself, you'll need to backtrack through the 2D array. Think about how you can use the values in the array to determine whether a character is included in the LCS.

editorial.title: Printing the Longest Common Subsequence
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the longest common subsequence (LCS) between two strings A and B. A key observation is that the LCS problem has optimal substructure, meaning the LCS of A and B can be constructed from the LCS of their prefixes.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible subsequences of A and B, then checking which ones are common. This approach is correct but results in an exponential time complexity due to the vast number of subsequences, making it impractical for strings of lengths up to 1000.
editorial.betterSolution: Dynamic programming offers a more efficient solution by storing the lengths of LCS for prefixes of A and B in a 2D array dp. The value at dp[i][j] represents the length of the LCS of the first i characters of A and the first j characters of B.
editorial.optimalSolution: The dynamic programming solution not only computes the length of the LCS but can also be used to print the LCS by backtracking from the bottom-right corner of the dp array. This approach is known to have a time complexity of O(N * M) and a space complexity of O(N * M), where N and M are the lengths of the strings A and B, respectively.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the dynamic programming solution stems from the fact that if A[i] == B[j], then the LCS of A[1..i] and B[1..j] is one character longer than the LCS of A[1..i-1] and B[1..j-1]. If A[i] != B[j], then the LCS of A[1..i] and B[1..j] is the maximum of the LCS of A[1..i-1] and B[1..j] and the LCS of A[1..i] and B[1..j-1]. This logic ensures that dp[i][j] always holds the length of the LCS of A[1..i] and B[1..j].
editorial.dryRun: For example, given A = 'ABC' and B = 'AC', the dp array would be constructed as follows: dp[0][0] = 0, dp[1][0] = 0, dp[0][1] = 0, dp[1][1] = 1 (because A[0] == B[0] = 'A'), dp[2][1] = 1, dp[1][2] = 1, dp[2][2] = 2. Backtracking from dp[2][2] yields the LCS 'AC'.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly initializing the dp array or the backtracking process.
Failing to consider the case when characters in A and B are equal.
Not properly handling the base cases for the dp array and backtracking.
Misinterpreting the dp array values as the actual LCS instead of its length.
Incorrectly constructing the LCS during backtracking.

functionSignature.java: public String printLCS(String A, String B);
functionSignature.python: def printLCS(self, A: str, B: str) -> str:
